beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Two particles, two boxes. How many ways can you arrange them? Your instinct says four. Quantum mechanics says: it depends on what kind of particle they are.

card.headline:
Why three particles in two states
give only one arrangement

beats.narration_text: Classical probability predicts four arrangements for two particles in two slots. Place quantum electrons in the same setup and the count drops to one. Why does counting change when the particles are identical fermions?

Start with two distinguishable classical particles — call them red and blue — and two states. Red in state A, blue in state B. Red in B, blue in A. Both in A. Both in B. Four distinct arrangements. This is Maxwell-Boltzmann counting. The particles are labeled, so every permutation is different.

Now make the particles identical — indistinguishable bosons. Red and blue become just... two particles. Swapping them creates no new microstate. The two mixed arrangements collapse into one. Three arrangements remain. This is Bose-Einstein counting.

Fermions go further. They are identical — same as bosons — but their wavefunction must be antisymmetric under particle exchange. Swap two fermions, the wavefunction flips sign. Put both in the same state, the wavefunction becomes its own negative — which means it must be zero. Double occupancy is literally forbidden.

Erase the double-occupancy configurations. Both-in-A: gone. Both-in-B: gone. The mixed arrangement is already collapsed to one by indistinguishability. One arrangement survives: one fermion in each state. That is the only microstate.

This is Pauli exclusion — stated as a counting rule, not a prohibition. The wavefunction antisymmetry doesn't forbid double occupancy by fiat. It makes the amplitude exactly zero. The state doesn't exist. Fermion counting is not a restriction on top of classical counting. It is a different kind of counting entirely.

The consequences are enormous. Electrons fill shells one per spin state. No two electrons can share the same quantum numbers. That is why matter has structure — why atoms have distinct sizes, why the periodic table has the shape it has, why metals conduct and insulators don't.

props.headline: The consequences are enormous. Electrons fill shells one per spin state.…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The consequences are enormous. Electrons fill shells one per spin state. No two electrons can share the same quantum numbers. That is why matter has structure —

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: take three particles and two spin states — spin up and spin down. For distinguishable classical particles the count is eight configurations. For identical bosons it shrinks to four. For fermions with two available states and three particles: impossible. Three fermions cannot fit in two states. That is Pauli — not a rule posted on the wall, but an arithmetic fact about antisymmetric wavefunctions.
Identical fermions: antisymmetric wavefunction makes double occupancy impossible. Classical four-way count collapses to one. Pauli exclusion is not a law — it is the number one.

card.headline:
Antisymmetric wavefunction
= zero amplitude for double occupancy
= Pauli exclusion = 1 microstate
